rank_note: Rank intentionally null. site/src/data/indexes/us-states.json contains only 21 of 51 jurisdictions; ranks 9-38 were lost in the original legacy-HTML extraction and the surviving entries were renumbered contiguously, so the displayed rank column is not a national rank. Missouri is currently displayed at rank 11 of 21, which is not a national rank and must not be treated as one. No rank should be assigned until the index is rebuilt with all 51 jurisdictions.
band_change: developing -> Developing (no band change). The composite moves from 25.0 to 27.5, which stays inside the Developing band (21-40).
delta_caveat: The published composite of 25.0 is a bulk-import placeholder with last_assessed null and no evidentiary basis. Idaho, Indiana, Missouri, North Dakota and South Dakota all carry byte-identical dimension values and the identical composite of exactly 25.0 — one invented number copied across five states. The score_delta of +2.5 and the band_change field therefore record the correction of a data defect. They do NOT represent institutional change in Missouri in either direction, and must not be published or interpreted as improvement or decline.
calibration_note: Not calibrated to the published us-states rank order, which is known to be corrupt, and not anchored to the placeholder value of 25.0. Calibrated to the methodology anchors and to the 17 states assessed at full depth under this methodology: Georgia 26.9, Iowa 30.6, Arizona 36.2, Ohio 38.1, North Carolina 45.0, Alaska 47.5, Oregon 50.6, Colorado 52.5, Wisconsin 53.1, Michigan 53.8, Delaware 53.8, Pennsylvania 54.4, Virginia 55.6, Maryland 55.6, New York 56.9, Illinois 60.0, New Jersey 60.6. Missouri at 27.5 sits just above Georgia 26.9 and below Iowa 30.6. Composite verified by running computeCompositeFromDimensions from site/scripts/lib/scoring.mjs (methodology v1.2). Verification output: {"composite":27.5,"band":"Developing","integrationPremium":0}. Inputs AWR 2.4, EMP 1.8, ACT 2.4, EQU 2.2, BND 1.8, ACC 1.8, SYS 2.4, INT 2.0. baseComposite 27.5; stdDev 0.2586 so consistencyMult 1.0; 8 of 8 dimensions below 4.0 so weaknessFactor 0 and integrationPremium 0.0; final composite 27.5, band Developing. The composite is 12.5 points from the nearest band boundary at 40, so the band is stable and no boundary sensitivity flag is required. Three sensitivity cases were also run through computeCompositeFromDimensions rather than computed by hand: declining the AB3 cross-state cap and flooring AB3 at 2 returns {"composite":26.9,"band":"Developing","integrationPremium":0}; raising EMP by a full 1.0 point returns {"composite":30.6,"band":"Developing","integrationPremium":0}; adding 0.5 to every dimension returns {"composite":40,"band":"Developing","integrationPremium":0}. Missouri therefore cannot reach the Functional band on any plausible near-term correction of a single dimension.
attribution_note: Scores Missouri state conduct only, across all branches and across both the Parson and Kehoe administrations, since the benchmark scores the jurisdiction rather than an administration. Missouri had unified Republican control of the governorship and both legislative chambers for the entire 2021-2026 window, so no divided-government attribution caveat applies. Actions of the independently elected Attorney General (Schmitt, Bailey, Hanaway) and the independently elected State Auditor (Fitzpatrick) are scored as state conduct, because both are statewide constitutional officers of the State of Missouri. Federal policy imposed on Missouri is excluded. The 2025 federal expansion of the Radiation Exposure Compensation Act covering St. Louis-area Coldwater Creek exposure is federal action and is NOT scored as state repair; it is cited under EQ5 only to establish that the compensation route was federal rather than state. The 2025 federal Rural Health Transformation Program award of 216 million dollars for fiscal 2026 is federal money, but the state-designed ToRCH Care allocation model built on it is state discretion and is scored. Federal Medicaid processing standards are federal, but Missouri exercised discretion in how it staffed and ran eligibility determination, which is what is scored under AC1 and EQ4. The 49-day federal disaster declaration delay after the May 2025 St. Louis tornado is federal and is not scored against Missouri; the state appropriation of 100 million dollars to the Disaster Relief Fund, signed 2025-06-14, is state conduct and is scored. Delays attributable to the City of St. Louis administration in tornado recovery are municipal and are excluded from this state assessment.

cross_state_consistency_flag.reasoning: Missouri meets both limbs of the rule. Limb one: broad executive resistance to public records access. The Missouri Supreme Court had to bar the Governor Office from charging attorney review time for Sunshine Law requests on 2021-06-29, and executive-branch fee and delay disputes have continued since. Limb two: an independently elected oversight body that is still publishing damaging, uncompelled findings. State Auditor Scott Fitzpatrick is elected statewide, published a federal-funds audit on 2025-09-30 containing 17 findings including a critical MO HealthNet oversight finding, publicly warned that Missouri is on track for emergency budget cuts, recommended tighter opioid settlement oversight in November 2025 after press reporting, and published an audit of former Governor Mike Parson own office on 2026-07-02 finding documentation gaps and policy violations. The Massachusetts output contingency is satisfied: the body is demonstrably still producing findings, so the cap of 3 holds rather than the floor of 2 returning.
cross_state_consistency_flag.scoring_effect: AB3 scored 3 under the cap. ACC dimension raw 1.8, composite 27.5, band Developing. Had the rule been declined and AB3 floored at 2, ACC would be raw 1.6 and the composite would be 26.9, band Developing. The rule is worth 0.6 composite points to Missouri and does not change the band either way.

key_evidence: Missouri voters approved Medicaid expansion by constitutional amendment in August 2020. The legislature excluded it from the fiscal 2022 budget and the governor withdrew the state plan amendment. The Missouri Supreme Court ruled unanimously on 2021-07-22 that the amendment was valid and cleared the way for enrollment.

Missouri voters approved Proposition A paid sick leave in November 2024 with nearly 1.7 million yes votes and 57.6 percent support. The legislature repealed it in May 2025, Governor Kehoe signed the repeal on 2025-07-10, and the repeal took effect on 2025-08-28. Two voter mandates overridden in five years makes this a pattern, not an incident.

Missouri had the worst Medicaid application delays in the United States. In February 2024 the median processing time was 77 days and 72 percent of applications exceeded the federal 45-day limit. The state had been under a CMS mitigation plan since July 2022, when average processing time reached 115 days.

A federal court ruled Missouri violated both federal SNAP law and the Americans with Disabilities Act. The court recorded an average call-center wait of 49 minutes 37 seconds, 50,488 queue deflections, 15,896 customer disconnects, and found 48.22 percent of applications rejected solely for failure to complete the interview.

Missouri executed Marcellus Williams on 2024-09-24 over the objection of both the St. Louis County Prosecuting Attorney office and the victim family. The Attorney General office also opposed the exonerations of Sandra Hemme, Christopher Dunn, Lamar Johnson and Kevin Strickland, and a state prison ignored a court order to release Dunn.

State Auditor Fitzpatrick published a federal-funds audit on 2025-09-30 with 17 findings, 13 of them repeated from prior Single Audits spanning one to five years, and stated that agencies have yet to implement previous recommendations.

Missouri child welfare frontline turnover was 40 percent at the end of fiscal 2025, down from 55 percent in September 2022. Between April and June 2025, no more than 6 percent of counties met the goal of monthly parent-child visits 60 percent of the time. The agency did not request additional staff.

Medicaid expansion is the largest compassion asset Missouri holds. Adult expansion enrollment peaked at 355,465 in August 2025 within a program covering more than 1.2 million residents, though rural uninsurance remained 9.9 percent against 6.9 percent in urban areas in 2024.

Missouri moved fast on disaster relief. The 2025-05-16 St. Louis tornado damaged or destroyed 16,000 structures, and Governor Kehoe signed a 100 million dollar Disaster Relief Fund appropriation on 2025-06-14, with housing aid targeted at families below 75 percent of area median income.

Missouri took control of the St. Louis police department away from the city in March 2025, joining Kansas City as the only major US metropolitan forces governed by a state board rather than local officials.

evidence.quote: Missouri Supreme Court upholds Medicaid expansion amendment
evidence.claim: the state refused to implement a voter-enacted constitutional amendment until ordered; primary support for I1 Consistency Under Pressure and AB2 Correction Willingness

evidence.quote: Missouri Attorney General Argues Lawmakers Can Refuse To Fund Medicaid Expansion
evidence.claim: the state legal position was that a voter-approved constitutional mandate need not be funded; supports I1 and I4

evidence.quote: Missouri voters expanded paid sick leave. The GOP undid it, and the repeal just took effect
evidence.claim: second independent instance of overriding a voter mandate, establishing a pattern rather than an incident; drives I1 and I4 to 1

evidence.quote: Missouri Gov. Kehoe signs bill repealing paid sick leave
evidence.claim: executive ratification of the repeal, dating state conduct precisely; supports E2 and EQ2

evidence.quote: As of February, Missouri 72% noncompliance rate stands far above other states
evidence.claim: worst-in-nation responsiveness failure sustained over years; primary support for AC1

evidence.quote: due to the persistent nature of the current backlog, we believe it is critical for the state to review its current processes and adopt additional alternative strategies that will mitigate the harm being caused to applicants
evidence.claim: federal regulator states the state is causing harm to applicants; supports AB2 and A5
evidence.source: Georgetown Center for Children and Families

evidence.quote: A shocking 48.22% of SNAP applications were rejected solely for failure to complete the interview and not based on any other qualifying factor
evidence.claim: people denied on procedure rather than eligibility, with no alternative offered; drives B4 Refusal Ethics and B3 Scope Clarity to 1 and EQ4 to 2
evidence.source: National Center for Law and Economic Justice

evidence.quote: Federal Court Rules Missouri Violated SNAP Law and the ADA
evidence.claim: independent judicial finding of both benefit-law and disability-accommodation violations; supports A2, B4 and EQ4
evidence.source: National Center for Law and Economic Justice

evidence.quote: Missouri Executes Marcellus Williams Despite Prosecutor Opposition
evidence.claim: execution proceeded over objections from the prosecuting office and the victim family; drives E4 Validation and AB1 Harm Acknowledgment to 1

evidence.quote: Missouri Exoneration Highlights State Opposition to Innocence Claims
evidence.claim: establishes a pattern rather than a single case; supports E4, AB1 and AB5

evidence.quote: A Missouri prison again has ignored an order to free a wrongfully convicted inmate
evidence.claim: executive-branch non-compliance with a judicial release order; supports AB1

evidence.quote: the majority of the findings in this report are similar to findings from past audits, which means many of the state agencies we review have yet to put our previous recommendations into place
evidence.claim: the state detects better than it corrects; drives AB4 Systemic Learning to 2 while supporting A3 and AB3 at 3

evidence.quote: State audit finds documentation gaps, policy violations in former Gov. Mike Parson office
evidence.claim: the independent oversight body is still producing damaging uncompelled findings, satisfying the Massachusetts output contingency on the AB3 rule

evidence.quote: Missouri Supreme Court: State cannot charge attorney fees for Sunshine Law requests
evidence.claim: executive-branch resistance to records access, the first limb of the AB3 cross-state rule

evidence.quote: Missouri pays 212M for prison health care, but inmate deaths are not a performance measure
evidence.claim: no outcome measurement in a major contract, and no statutory duty to report deaths in custody; supports AC3, A1 and I2

evidence.quote: Missouri lawmakers push for oversight panel to monitor conditions in state prisons
evidence.claim: no independent prison oversight body exists; supports A4 Signal Amplification at 2

evidence.quote: turnover among frontline division staff, which include investigators, foster care case managers and hotline workers, was 40% at the end of fiscal year 2025, down from 55% in September 2022
evidence.claim: turnover tracked and structurally addressed but still severe; supports B1 at 3 and AC4 at 3

evidence.quote: enrollment in the Adult Expansion Group peaked again in August 2025 at 355,465, the highest level since the expansion started
evidence.claim: the single largest coverage gain in the assessment window; primary support for EQ1 at 3 and I5 at 3

evidence.quote: Black women in Missouri 2.5 times more likely to die from pregnancy-related causes compared to white women
evidence.claim: the state publishes its own disparity honestly and acts on it; primary support for EQ3 at 3 and E3

evidence.quote: The Missouri Optimizing Postpartum Care Task Force was established in 2024
evidence.claim: a working detect-investigate-correct loop in maternal health; supports AB4, E2 and S5

evidence.quote: lawmakers added 100 million in general revenue funding to the state Disaster Relief Fund
evidence.claim: fast, need-targeted state disaster response within four weeks of the tornado; mitigating evidence under AC1 and primary support for AC2

evidence.quote: Gov. Mike Kehoe signs bill to put St. Louis police under state control
evidence.claim: removal of municipal self-determination; primary support for B2 Autonomy Preservation at 2

evidence.quote: Missouri set to enact gerrymandered map despite 300K signatures for repeal referendum
evidence.claim: narrowing rather than examining the structures through which people seek change; drives S4 to 1

evidence.quote: Missouri House advances plan to make it harder for some amendments to pass
evidence.claim: legislative restriction of the citizen initiative channel; supports I4 at 1 and S4 at 1

evidence.quote: Missouri currently has 20 behavioral health centers in the mobile crisis response network statewide
evidence.claim: genuine upstream and cross-system investment; primary support for S1 and S3 at 3 and mitigating evidence under E1

evidence.quote: the state has no process for holding local governments accountable on their spending, and no agency is dedicated to enforcing the law
evidence.claim: opioid settlement funds distributed without efficacy oversight; supports AC3 at 2 and AC5 at 2

evidence.quote: Missouri Medicaid expansion is nearing a funding cliff few Missourians realize
evidence.claim: no published long-horizon plan for the largest coverage program in the state; supports S2 at 2 and A5 at 2

evidence.quote: The years-of-service pay plan provides a 1% pay differential for eligible state employees for every two years of continuous state service, up to a maximum of 10%
evidence.claim: material investment in the state own workforce; primary support for I3 at 3 and B1 at 3
